summary: MoE architecture inverts hardware logic — unified memory (Mac M4 Pro 64GB) beats discrete GPU for 120B/12B-active model deployment
No 50M model exists off-the-shelf; SmolLM2-135M is the pragmatic micro, Phi-4-mini/Qwen3.5-4B for nano, Nemotron 3 Super for super
Obsidian vault serves as shared memory bus via 3 layers (embeddings, MCP read/write, graph RAG) mapped to 3 model tiers
Three-stage memory pipeline (capture→process→inject) using hooks, processing in the swarm not in Claude's context window
Ollama alone handles 3-model serving for single user — no custom orchestration needed yet, phased complexity path
Daily retraining is a 5-15 min LoRA adapter swap (~3MB), not model training. RAG handles recall (80%), LoRA handles personality (20%)
135M micro is a bouncer (classify, route, retrieve), not a bartender (explain, teach). Two-layer split on M4 16GB at ~9GB total
Link-to-learning needs 14B on PC 64GB — comprehension + context mapping + pedagogical synthesis. CPU-only at 2-6 tok/s is fine for batch
Micro retrains daily for $0 locally, 14B fine-tunes monthly for $1-6 on RunPod, nano never needs fine-tuning. Total: ~$3-6/month
Two cheap machines beat one expensive one — Mac Mini (3-5W sidecar) + PC (on-demand compute). Syncthing + Ollama API connect them
Handoff pattern is the right topology for 3-model personal swarm — one Nemoclaw (micro as router), not agent-per-model
The swarm is already event-driven — filesystem/Syncthing/hooks IS the event bus, no message broker needed
Hour-by-hour walkthrough of what Nemoclaw+swarm does daily — morning briefing, coding sidecar, link-to-learning, daily maintenance
70% of Nemoclaw is deterministic code, not AI. Build plumbing first, add intelligence gradually.
Honest cost/capability accounting: local swarm gives persistent memory and privacy, not better reasoning
